entities.description: The 117,344-byte DLPSTEXTCLASS XML artifact used by this repository, pinned through the CME git submodule. It represents 2,346 verse lines plus a separately encoded explicit as numbered XML line 2347.
entities.label: General Corpus pinned repository copy of Ipomydon.xml
notes: The XML has 10 DIV1 and 10 LG containers supplied for navigation, 2,347 L elements, 31 folio milestones, and 83 page milestones. The containers are not ten authorial parts.
The final L element is the explicit, not a 2,347th verse line.

bibliographic.citation: The Lyfe of Ipomydon, ed. Tadahiro Ikegami, electronic text U-658-A (Oxford Text Archive / University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative, March 1996).

entities.description: The U-M Humanities Text Initiative representation published in March 1996. Its header names Ikegami's 1983 edition, omits front and back matter plus printed editorial notes, and states that large structural divisions were supplied only for navigation.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME encoding Ipomydon / OTA U-658-A
notes: The inherited header credits Hue de Rotelande as author, but the encoded text is the anonymous Middle English B version.

After normalization of OTA legacy thorn/yogh codes, angle-bracket insertion markers, and whitespace, comparison with harley2252-0658.txt found only two localized differences across 2,347 numbered positions; this confirms the immediate electronic source at high confidence.

entities.description: A legacy OTA deposit now exposed by the Linguistic Linked Data Service. It contains three ASCII text streams: the complete Harley manuscript version and transcriptions of two incomplete de Worde prints. The current landing applies CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 but carries inherited creator and date fields that do not accurately describe the Middle English B work or payload chronology.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive deposit 0658
notes: The current landing's creator Hue and created 1170-1190 fields belong to the Anglo-Norman source author/work and are not target-layer metadata.
The landing's issued 1976 field is incompatible with source volumes dated 1983 and 1985 and with the files' 1986 processing records; its origin remains unexplained.
The current migrated header is malformed at an authority/distributor boundary. The cached legacy Bodleian header is well formed and preserves the same source and file-map statements.

rights.basis: The current official OTA/LLDS record applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0.
rights.component: Current OTA 0658 deposit, three distributed text bitstreams, and repository package
notes: The license is recorded at the OTA digital-deposit layer. It does not independently license the underlying Seijo editions, manuscript, early prints, or third-party photographs.

entities.description: The complete deposited ASCII representation of the Harley MS 2252 B text, with numbered positions 1-2347. Positions 1-2346 are verse lines and position 2347 is the explicit. Legacy character codes and angle brackets mark thorn/yogh and supplied text.
entities.label: OTA 0658 harley2252 text stream

entities.description: The deposited transcription of the one-leaf British Library de Worde fragment under its legacy c. 1522 filename. It labels positions 261-320; four dotted missing positions (289-292) leave 56 extant verbal lines.
entities.label: OTA 0658 worde1522 text stream
notes: The filename preserves Ikegami-era dating and is not treated as resolving current print chronology.

entities.description: The deposited transcription of the incomplete Morgan de Worde copy under its legacy c. 1530 filename. It contains numbered positions 193-2346 and lacks the opening represented by quire A.
entities.label: OTA 0658 worde30 text stream
notes: The current Morgan catalog dates the physical copy approximately 1520-1524; the OTA filename does not supersede that catalog evidence.

bibliographic.citation: Tadahiro Ikegami, ed., The Lyfe of Ipomydon, vol. I: Text and Introduction, Seijo English Monographs 21 (Tokyo: Department of English, Seijo University, 1983), lxvi + 86 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Lyfe of Ipomydon

entities.description: The immediate scholarly edition behind the Harley text stream. Ikegami presents a conservative transcription of Harley MS 2252, ff. 54r-84r, retains manuscript spelling, silently expands abbreviations and numerals, supplies punctuation and word division, and marks emendation or restoration with brackets.
entities.label: Tadahiro Ikegami, The Lyfe of Ipomydon, volume I (1983)
notes: The edition prints the 2,346-line B text on pp. 1-84 and a two-page appendix transcribing the de Worde leaf on pp. 85-86.
Its selected manuscript plate of f. 57r and watermark photographs are partial supporting images, not a complete Harley MS 2252 facsimile.

rights.basis: The volume states copyright by the Department of English, Seijo University, 1983, and no open license was found on the official repository record.
rights.component: Ikegami's 1983 edited text, introduction, apparatus, notes, selection, and typographic presentation
notes: The medieval B work is a separate historical layer; this record does not treat public access to the PDF as permission to redistribute the modern edition.

entities.description: A scholarly edition of the two incomplete early de Worde print survivors. The British Library leaf was transcribed from the original; the Morgan text was prepared from microfilm and checked against the original. The volume also compares those print readings with Harley MS 2252.
entities.label: Tadahiro Ikegami, The Lyfe of Ipomydon, volume II (1985)
notes: Ikegami's c. 1522 and c. 1530-1531 labels are preserved as historical editorial claims. Current catalogs and later scholarship give different date ranges and dispute ordering.

entities.description: The composite Commonplace Book of John Colyns. The unique manuscript copy of Ipomydon B occupies ff. 54r-84r and was copied in the second half of the fifteenth century; Colyns acquired the book in 1517 and compiled the composite volume in the early sixteenth century.

holding.notes: The exact item record identifies Ipomydon B at ff. 54r-84r and calls it a unique copy.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2252

physicalDescription.extent: One composite volume; ff. i + 133* + 166, with additional unfoliated flyleaves and pastedowns
notes: The target text is copied in single columns across ff. 54r-84r within the first poetic booklet.
The current catalog describes English Gothic cursive scripts, Secretary with Anglicana features; Ikegami discusses the target booklet and a localized hand change on f. 83v.

rights.basis: The target manuscript copy and historical content were created in the fifteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.
rights.component: Fifteenth-century manuscript text, script, and historical content

access.accessMethod: Use the British Library digitisation form, identify Harley MS 2252, request a complete color surrogate of the whole physical manuscript where feasible, and list covers/boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and every manuscript leaf; identify ff. 54r-84r separately as the work portion. The Library will quote after conservation and existing-surrogate checks.
access.entityLabel: British Library digital-image object for Harley MS 2252
access.format: Existing surrogate scan or new digital photography; quote required
notes: Original manuscripts cannot normally be flatbed scanned; new photography may be required unless an existing surrogate can be supplied.
Publication or redistribution permission is separate from obtaining the files.

entities.description: The sole known physical fragment of one de Worde print state, preserved in the Bagford Ballads. The schema has no historical-print type, so this physical early-print survivor is represented provisionally as a scholarly-edition entity.

entities.description: The only recorded copy of the longer de Worde print state. It lacks quire A but preserves signatures B-H4 I6 and text corresponding to manuscript positions 193-2346, ending with Robert Copland's printer's envoi. The schema has no historical-print type, so this physical early-print survivor is represented provisionally as a scholarly-edition entity.

holding.notes: The current Morgan record says this is the only recorded copy, missing quire A.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde Ipomydon, Morgan PML 20896.2
notes: The current Morgan record's Hue creator heading is inherited source-work metadata. Its own notes correctly state that this print is an English translation/condensation and that Copland's envoi does not make him the adapter.
The Morgan record calls this possibly the earliest surviving edition, while Mayer's account of Sánchez-Martí describes the Morgan copy as later than the Bagford state. The discrepancy remains open.

next_steps: Ask the Oxford Text Archive to inspect legacy accession logs for item 0658.
Preserve 22 April 1986 only as an embedded file-processing timestamp unless deposit documentation establishes more.
openQuestions.question: What original deposit or creation date should replace OTA 0658's impossible 1976 issue field?

next_steps: Consult Sánchez-Martí's complete 2005 argument and current ESTC/USTC records together with the two physical survivors.
Do not assert a print-to-print direction until bibliographical evidence resolves the conflict between the Morgan record, Mayer's summary, and Ikegami.
openQuestions.question: Which de Worde state was printed first, what are the best current date ranges, and was either state set through the other rather than directly from Harley MS 2252?

next_steps: Monitor the exact catalog's digitised-content status and test a provider-published image manifest when service is restored.
Use the digitisation form to request a complete color surrogate of the whole physical manuscript, naming all exterior and interior surfaces and mapping ff. 54r-84r separately.
Obtain written publication/redistribution terms before placing any supplied images in Git or print-on-demand outputs.
openQuestions.question: When will the British Library's existing Harley MS 2252 image object become publicly available again, and did its published inventory cover the complete physical manuscript?

next_steps: Ask U-M and OTA to clarify which electronic layer each statement governs and whether they had authority to license inherited editorial readings.
Keep the Seijo PDFs private and treat OTA's Creative Commons license as applying only to the digital deposit unless providers confirm broader scope.
openQuestions.question: How should users reconcile the legacy personal-scholarly-use condition in U-M's XML with the current OTA deposit's CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 label and protected Seijo source edition?

next_steps: Use the British Library or EEBO route for the Bagford leaf and ask which image terms apply.
Request a quote from the Morgan for every surviving page of PML 20896.2 and secure separate online/publication permission.
Keep Ikegami volume II's four selected plates labeled partial.

reviewNotes: Hue de Rotelande is the author of the late-twelfth-century Anglo-Norman source work, not the author of this anonymous Middle English B version; inherited U-M, OTA, and Morgan creator headings are not treated as target-work responsibility.
The primary text path is repository artifact -> U-M encoding -> OTA harley2252 stream -> Ikegami volume I -> Harley MS 2252, ff. 54r-84r.
OTA item 0658 is modeled as a deposit containing three distinct text streams. Its two early-print streams support print transmission but are not upstream of the repository artifact.

The OTA landing's 1976 issue date cannot describe these payloads: Ikegami's source volumes appeared in 1983 and 1985, and all three files contain embedded processing timestamps from 22 April 1986. Those timestamps establish file processing, not the exact OTA deposit date.

The current British Library record links a digital-image object but explicitly marks its images unavailable. No current public whole-manuscript image inventory could be audited; selected plates inside the Seijo volumes remain partial manuscript or print reproductions.
Current scholarship and catalogs disagree about the order and dates of the two de Worde editions. No print-to-print direction is asserted.
Local caching records research availability only. It does not authorize redistribution of the Seijo PDFs, British Library or Morgan images, or any other protected layer.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local pinned copy of the University of Michigan CME encoding of the anonymous Middle English B version of The Lyfe of Ipomydon. File comparison shows that U-M transformed the Oxford Text Archive's harley2252-0658 text stream, preserving its numbering and readings apart from two localized differences; OTA documentation says that stream was transcribed from Tadahiro Ikegami's 1983 Seijo edition, and Ikegami transcribed the unique copy in British Library Harley MS 2252, ff. 54r-84r. The same OTA deposit also preserves separate transcriptions of two fragmentary Wynkyn de Worde print witnesses from Ikegami's 1985 volume. Complete official PDFs of both Seijo volumes, the three OTA payloads and package, and Nora Mayer's later thesis edition are cached locally. The British Library's catalog-linked manuscript images are currently unavailable, but a complete-manuscript surrogate can be requested through its paid digitisation service.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-adapter
catalogSummary.date: Probably the second half of the fifteenth century
catalogSummary.title: The Lyfe of Ipomydon

catalogingSubject.label: The Lyfe of Ipomydon
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English B version of Ipomydon: one 2,346-line verse romance independently translated, condensed, and adapted from Hue de Rotelande's late-twelfth-century Anglo-Norman Ipomedon. The work is distinct from the tail-rhyme A version, prose C version, its unique Harley MS 2252 copy, the two de Worde print states, Ikegami's 1983 and 1985 editions, the OTA/U-M encodings, later scholarship, and repository XML. The ten DIV1/LG containers in the XML are supplied navigation, not ten constituent works or authorial sections.

contentParts.label: The Lyfe of Ipomydon
notes: The ten XML LG containers do not correspond to stanzas or evidenced authorial divisions; verse_groups is therefore unknown/null rather than ten.
The prose-like explicit is paratext and does not make the work mixed-form.
contentParts.summary: Complete 2,346-line B-version romance from the appeal to listeners through Ipomydon's upbringing, disguised service and tournaments, departure and return as a fool, rescue of the lady, fraternal recognition, revelation, marriage, settlement, and terminal prayer.

assertion.notes: Ikegami argues for post-1400 and probably the latter half of the fifteenth century; Mayer's phonological analysis supports the same probable half-century.

dateStatements.display: Probably the second half of the fifteenth century
notes: This dates the anonymous B work, not Hue's twelfth-century source, the early-sixteenth-century composite volume, or the 1983 edition.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century in British Library Harley MS 2252
notes: Harley 2252 is the unique manuscript copy of the B version. This does not imply that its surviving copy is the author's holograph.

assertion.notes: The Morgan record dates PML 20896.2 approximately 1520-1524 and calls it possibly earliest; traditional records date the Bagford state c. 1522, but scholarly accounts disagree about ordering.

dateStatements.display: By approximately 1520-1524 under the current Morgan dating; exact print order and dates remain disputed
notes: No single source-witness binding is attached because the earliest surviving state remains contested.

extent.description: One complete 2,346-line narrative poem in rhyming couplets, followed by an explicit. The repository XML has 10 supplied DIV1 and 10 supplied LG navigation containers, 2,347 L elements, 31 folio milestones, and 83 page milestones. XML line 2347 is the explicit; those encoding structures do not make the work ten sections, 2,347 verse lines, or mixed prose and verse.

formStatements.description: A 2,346-line narrative romance in rhyming couplets, followed by a brief explicit. The explicit and supplied navigation do not make the work mixed-form.

genres.label: Middle English romance
notes: Mayer describes B as a popular romance; the normalized corpus genre uses the established broader label Middle English romance.

genres.label: Chivalric romance
notes: Knightly service, tournament victories, combat, concealed identity, honor, inheritance, and marriage structure the narrative.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English introductions and catalog metadata belong to edition or digital layers and do not make the medieval work bilingual.
Hue de Rotelande's name in legacy U-M/OTA and Morgan creator fields belongs to the source-work layer. The target B version remains anonymous.
Robert Copland's surviving envoi supports a printer or print-shop role in one early edition, not authorship or adaptation of the medieval B text.
The current British Library catalog's DIMEV 3462-1 is retained as the active occurrence identifier; historical IMEV/NIMEV 2142 remains separately recorded for older scholarship.
Rights and access differ across the medieval work, manuscript, early prints, Ikegami editions, electronic encodings, and page images; see the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review post-Ikegami dialect and localization scholarship using current corpus methods.
Keep dialect localization distinct from manuscript-copy origin, London ownership, and London printing.
openQuestions.question: Can the B version's composition date and place be narrowed beyond a probable second half of the fifteenth century and North-East Midlands linguistic region?

next_steps: Consult detailed comparative studies of Hue's Ipomedon and the A, B, and C Middle English renditions.
Do not substitute a surviving modern edition or representative Anglo-Norman witness for the unidentified historical exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Anglo-Norman exemplar or textual state did the anonymous B translator-adapter use?

next_steps: Reconcile current Morgan, ESTC/USTC, Sánchez-Martí, Ikegami, and Bagford evidence.
Retain the broad current Morgan approximately 1520-1524 range as tentative discovery metadata without asserting a print-to-print direction.
openQuestions.question: Which de Worde state is the earliest surviving publication, and what date should be preferred for each?

notes: The English language, manuscript origin, and early transmission support broad cultural origin; this is not a city-level composition claim.

assertion.notes: The localization is a scholarly inference from linguistic evidence, not a colophon statement.

notes: Do not transfer the British Library's broad manuscript origin England or Colyns's later London ownership into a more precise authorial place.
London is the de Worde imprint place and Colyns transmission context, not an asserted place of medieval composition.

scope.description: Early printed transmission by Wynkyn de Worde

assertion.notes: Both modern sources distinguish the B text from Hue's source work and from the other two Middle English renditions.

notes: Hue de Rotelande is not assigned target-work authorship.

scope.description: Independent Middle English rendering of Hue de Rotelande's Anglo-Norman Ipomedon

notes: Translator and adapter describe two functions of the same unknown responsibility, not two known people.

scope.description: Substantial condensation and reshaping into a 2,346-line Middle English popular romance
Immediate scholarly source edition, The Lyfe of Ipomydon, volume I (1983)

notes: This edition-level role does not make Ikegami an author of the medieval romance.

scope.description: Conservative edited transcription from Harley MS 2252, ff. 54r-84r
Comparison of manuscript readings and corrections with earlier Kölbing and Weber editions

subjects.label: Chivalric service, combat, hunting, and tournament
Kinship, inheritance, and reunion
notes: The hero's royal inheritance and recognition of his half-brother Campanus/Capanius converge with the final marriage and settlement.

subjects.label: Manuscript-to-print transmission
notes: Harley MS 2252 was prepared for early print use; the exact order and directness of the two de Worde states remain disputed.

summaries.value: Ipomydon, heir to Apulia, hears of the beauty and prowess of the lady who rules Calabria and enters her service without revealing his royal identity. He excels in hunting and wins a great tournament while appearing on successive days in white, red, and black, yet conceals his victories and departs before accepting marriage. When the lady is later threatened by Duke Geron, Ipomydon returns disguised as a fool, secures the right to answer her appeal for aid, defends her messenger from three attackers, defeats Geron, and is temporarily mistaken for him. A combat leads to recognition of his long-lost half-brother Campanus; Ipomydon's disguises and earlier deeds are revealed, he reunites with the lady, and the romance closes with marriage, kingship, rewards, and a prayer for salvation.

This record catalogs the anonymous Middle English B work, not Hue de Rotelande's Anglo-Norman source, another Middle English version, a manuscript or early-print carrier, Ikegami's editions, the OTA/U-M encodings, or the repository file. The XML's ten large DIV1/LG units are supplied navigation. The verse extent is 2,346 lines; XML L 2347 is the explicit and is excluded from the verse-line count.

notes: Established title of the B version and the immediate edition; spelling preserves the edition's Lyfe and Ipomydon forms.
The version label distinguishes this short-couplet text from the tail-rhyme A and prose C Middle English renditions.
Normalized from the repository XML's bracketed-expansion representation and not counted as a 2,347th verse line.

work.summary: Ipomydon, heir to Apulia, hears of the beauty and prowess of the lady who rules Calabria and enters her service without revealing his royal identity. He excels in hunting and wins a great tournament while appearing on successive days in white, red, and black, yet conceals his victories and departs before accepting marriage. When the lady is later threatened by Duke Geron, Ipomydon returns disguised as a fool, secures the right to answer her appeal for aid, defends her messenger from three attackers, defeats Geron, and is temporarily mistaken for him. A combat leads to recognition of his long-lost half-brother Campanus; Ipomydon's disguises and earlier deeds are revealed, he reunites with the lady, and the romance closes with marriage, kingship, rewards, and a prayer for salvation.
